All Questions
Were You Asked This?
Design an Ad Server

Design an ad server system that delivers targeted advertisements to users based on their preferences and behavior, while optimizing for performance and revenue.

Asked at:

Amazon

Spotify

Google

An ad server is a system that selects and delivers personalized advertisements in real time when a web page or app requests an ad slot. Think Google Ads or Meta Ads: advertisers define campaigns and bids, publishers embed ad tags, and the ad server decides which ad to show to which user while keeping latency extremely low and optimizing revenue.

Interviewers ask this because ad serving combines hard, real-world constraints: sub-100ms decisioning, personalization, auctions and pricing, budget and frequency controls, high-throughput logging, and fraud/attribution pipelines. A strong answer shows you can balance latency versus accuracy, consistency versus availability, and short-term revenue versus long-term system health, while decomposing a multi-stage decision flow into robust, scalable services.

Community Solutions
Design an Ad Server
by XenogeneicVioletBass404
0
3
Design Ad Server
by DramaticAquaBison294
0
1
Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Advertisers should be able to create campaigns with targeting rules (e.g., audience segments, geo, device) and bids/budgets.

Publishers should be able to request an ad for a placement and receive a relevant creative with low latency.

Users should be shown high-quality, policy-compliant, and frequency-capped ads tailored to their preferences and behavior.

The system should track impressions, clicks, and conversions to optimize delivery and pacing toward advertiser budgets and revenue goals.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How do you achieve sub-100ms end-to-end ad decisioning at p99 while maintaining high relevance?

How would you design the auction, pricing, and pacing to maximize revenue without overserving or starving campaigns?

How do you enforce global budgets and user-level frequency caps across a distributed fleet without hot keys or overspend?

How will you build the logging and attribution pipeline (impressions/clicks/conversions) to optimize models while keeping storage and costs in check?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Scaling Reads

Ad serving is a read-heavy, latency-critical path: targeting indices, user segments, policy lists, and candidate creatives must be served from memory and edge caches. Using read replicas, in-memory stores, and precomputed indices keeps the hot path fast and resilient.

Read More
Dealing with Contention

Budgets and frequency caps create hot counters. Without sharding, batching, and approximate prechecks, you get thundering herds, hot keys, and overspend. Contention-aware designs ensure consistency is good enough while preserving throughput.

Read More
Multi-step Processes

An ad request triggers a multi-stage flow: eligibility, policy checks, auction, pricing, pacing, and logging/billing. Treating this as a durable, well-ordered pipeline with compensations (e.g., idempotent logging, late reconciliation) prevents revenue and compliance errors.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Redis

Redis provides ultra-low-latency access for targeting indices, feature caches, and sharded counters for budgets and frequency caps. Its data structures (sets, sorted sets, bitmaps) fit candidate lookup and per-user cap checks on the hot path.

Read More
Kafka

Kafka is well-suited for high-throughput, durable streams of impressions, clicks, and conversions, enabling idempotent processing, deduplication, and real-time aggregation for pacing, reporting, and model training.

Read More
Cassandra

Cassandra handles time-series writes and large-scale aggregates for attribution and reporting with high availability and predictable performance, making it a strong fit for storing ad events and per-time-slice rollups.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

Ad Click Aggregator

Both systems require reliable ingestion, deduplication, and aggregation of high-volume ad events, with idempotency and time-windowed metrics critical for billing and optimization.

Read
Practice
FB News Feed

Both perform personalized, low-latency ranking on the hot path backed by large precomputed indices and caches, and both balance freshness, relevance, and tail-latency SLAs.

Read
Practice
Online Auction

Ad serving hinges on running auctions at scale with pricing rules, floors, and fairness. The auction problem maps closely to bidding strategies, pacing, and winner determination under strict latency budgets.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Hitting a transactional database on the hot path for targeting and counters

Disk-bound queries will blow the p99 latency budget and cause timeouts under load. An ad server must rely on in-memory indices and sharded counters to meet sub-100ms decisioning.

Ignoring distributed budget and frequency cap consistency

Without a plan for sharded counters, approximate prechecks, and idempotent updates, the system will overspend budgets or violate caps, creating revenue leakage and advertiser mistrust.

No clear auction, pricing, and pacing model

Selecting the highest bid without floors, pacing, or policy checks sacrifices revenue optimization and fairness, can front-load spend, and can violate brand safety requirements.
